risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants who chose the higher intensity intervention (MTS or FFS-E) were scheduled to attend their first class where they were randomized to MTS or FFS-E and immediately began treatment." Comment: The study states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated. There is no explicit random method (e.g., computer-generated sequence, random numbers, blocks, or similar) and no non-random method is stated, so the sequence generation is insufficiently described.

_evidence.method_quote: Participants who chose the higher intensity intervention (MTS or FFS-E) were scheduled to attend their first class where they were randomized to MTS or FFS-E and immediately began treatment.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants choosing the higher-intensity intervention were randomized to MTS or FFS-E at the first class, but the sequence generation method is not described.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants who chose the higher intensity intervention (MTS or FFS-E) were scheduled to attend their first class where they were randomized to MTS or FFS-E and immediately began treatment." Comment: The article describes randomization, but it does not state any concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, identical containers) that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment. Under Cochrane criteria, absence of concealment information is Unclear risk, not Low or High.

_evidence.note: The article describes randomization to MTS or FFS-E but does not state how the allocation sequence was concealed from recruiters.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "participants were asked to choose whether they wished to be placed in a low intensity (QL) or high intensity (MTS /FFS-E) intervention" and "Participants who chose the higher intensity intervention (MTS or FFS-E) were scheduled to attend their first class where they were randomized to MTS or FFS-E and immediately began treatment." Comment: Participants clearly knew which intervention intensity they were choosing, and the study text does not describe any blinding of participants or personnel delivering MTS/FFS-E. Because several reported outcomes were subjective/self-reported (e.g. urge ratings, FFMQ, AAQ, PSS, class attendance, practice logs, smoking days), knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes, so performance bias is high.

_evidence.blinding_quote: At the orientation meeting, potential participants received an overview of two high intensity interventions (MTS /FFS-E) and a lower intensity Quit Line (QL) intervention that used the Wisconsin Tobacco Quit Line (WTQL) as its behavioral therapy. At the orientation, the WTQL was described as “the most widely used and studied smoking cessation intervention in the state,” FFS-E as an “enhanced version of a well-studied, widely used intervention”, and MTS as a “novel and unproven intervention.” Potential participants were told that all interventions provided equal payment and cessation medications, and were asked to choose whether they wished to be placed in a low intensity (QL) or high intensity (MTS /FFS-E) intervention. Participants who chose the higher intensity intervention (MTS or FFS-E) were scheduled to attend their first class where they were randomized to MTS or FFS-E and immediately began treatment.
outcome_categories: number of days smoked in the first two weeks post-quit
subjective_outcomes: number of days smoked in the first two weeks post-quit

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Smoking status and self-report data were collected for all groups at three assessment visits" and "Primary outcome measures were 7-day point-prevalence abstinence at 4- and 24-weeks post-quit day, confirmed by a carbon monoxide (CO) breath test and Timeline Follow-Back smoking calendar (TLFB)..." Comment: The study used patient-reported outcomes (TLFB, urge ratings, FFMQ, AAQ, PSS, Meditation/Relaxation Calendar), so the patient was the assessor for several outcomes. The article does not state that participants/outcome assessors were blinded. Because unblinded self-report outcomes are highly vulnerable to detection bias, and the study reports a mix of objective and subjective outcomes, the worst-outcome rule makes the domain High risk.

outcomes.label: number of days smoked in the first two weeks post-quit

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Intent-to-treat analysis of 7-day point prevalence abstinence between two groups was almost identical at 4 weeks..." and "An intent-to-treat analysis that carried forward baseline scores for participants who did not attend follow-up visits (MTS n = 68, FFS-E = 67, QL = 40) showed no significant differences between MTS and FFS-E at 24-weeks post-quit." Comment: The primary abstinence outcome was analyzed by ITT, which is reassuring, but the report does not provide per-arm numbers actually observed vs missing for the primary outcome time points or reasons for loss to follow-up on that outcome. The extracted flow information reports assessment-visit attrition percentages (4-week: MTS 39.7%, FFS-E 32.8%; 24-week: MTS 57.4%, FFS-E 55.2%), but not direct analysed-outcome denominators/reasons sufficient to determine whether attrition biased the primary comparison. Under Cochrane guidance this is best judged Unclear rather than High when detailed attrition data are not fully reported.

per_arm_flow.reasons: Intent-to-treat analysis of 7-day point prevalence abstinence; attrition at 4-week assessment visit: 39.7%; at 24-week assessment visit: 57.4%.
Intent-to-treat analysis of 7-day point prevalence abstinence; attrition at 4-week assessment visit: 32.8%; at 24-week assessment visit: 55.2%.
Intent-to-treat analysis includes QL in the self-report measures table; attrition at 4-week assessment visit: 35.5%; at 24-week assessment visit: 60%.

_evidence.missing_data_method: ITT; report completers only for repeated self-report measures; intent-to-treat analysis that carried forward baseline scores for participants who did not attend follow-up visits
_evidence.notes: CONSORT-style flow is partly described in text and tables, but per-arm lost numbers at the analysed outcome time points are not directly reported. Attrition percentages are reported for assessment visits; the primary outcome analysis is described as ITT.
